header.title: Hash Table
header.description: A hash table maps keys to values using a hash function that converts any key into an array index. It achieves O(1) average-case insert, search, and delete — the most efficient lookup structure for unsorted data.

howItWorks.title: How hash tables work
items: A hash function transforms a key into an integer index: hash(key) % capacity. A good hash function distributes keys uniformly so each bucket receives approximately n/capacity keys.
Collisions are inevitable — by the pigeonhole principle, if you insert more keys than buckets, two keys must share a bucket. The collision resolution strategy determines how the table handles this.
Separate chaining stores a linked list in each bucket. Collisions append to the list. Lookup scans the list linearly — O(chain length) = O(load factor) on average.
Open addressing stores all entries directly in the array. On collision, it probes for the next available slot. Linear probing checks (hash + 1) % n, (hash + 2) % n, etc.

Load factor = entries / capacity. As it approaches 1, chains grow longer or probe sequences lengthen and performance degrades to O(n). Tables resize (rehash) when load exceeds a threshold — typically 0.75 for chaining, 0.7 for probing.
Resize doubles the capacity and rehashes every entry into the new array — O(n) work. But since it happens every O(n) inserts, the amortized cost per insert stays O(1).

variants.label: Separate Chaining
variants.description: Each bucket holds a linked list of all entries that hash to it. Collisions grow the list. The table never becomes full — chains can grow unbounded, but performance degrades with high load.
characteristics: Load factor can exceed 1.0 — no hard capacity limit
Simple deletion — splice the node from the chain
Cache-unfriendly — linked list nodes scattered in memory
Java HashMap and Python dict use variants of chaining
useCases: Python dict (hash table + open addressing hybrid)

variants.label: Open Addressing — Linear Probing
variants.description: All entries live in the flat array. On collision, probe forward: (hash + i) % capacity. Better cache locality than chaining but suffers from primary clustering — runs of occupied slots form and grow.
characteristics: All data in one contiguous array — cache friendly
Load factor must stay below ~0.7 to avoid long probe chains
Deletion requires tombstones — setting to null would break probe sequences
Primary clustering: nearby collisions cluster together, worsening future probes

variants.label: Open Addressing — Quadratic Probing
variants.description: Same as linear probing but probe offset grows quadratically. Reduces primary clustering — nearby keys no longer compete for the same probe sequence. Secondary clustering still possible.
characteristics: Probe: (hash + i²) % capacity for i = 0, 1, 2...
Eliminates primary clustering — different initial positions get different sequences
Secondary clustering: keys with same hash still share the full probe sequence
Only guaranteed to find an empty slot if capacity is prime and load < 0.5
useCases: Variant of open addressing for reduced clustering
Hash tables where primary clustering is observed in practice

variants.label: Double Hashing
variants.structure:
probe(i) = (hash1(key) + i × hash2(key)) % capacity
hash2 must never return 0
variants.description: Uses a second hash function to determine the probe step size. Each key gets its own unique probe sequence — eliminates both primary and secondary clustering. Best open addressing strategy in practice.
characteristics: Probe: (h1(key) + i × h2(key)) % capacity
h2 must be coprime with capacity — use h2(k) = p - (k % p) where p is prime < capacity
Eliminates clustering — each key has a unique probe sequence
Harder to implement correctly but superior performance at high load
useCases: When open addressing is required but linear probing clusters too much

variants.label: Robin Hood Hashing
variants.structure:
On collision, steal slot from the key with shorter probe distance.
Result: all probe distances are more uniform.
variants.description: A variation of linear probing where on collision, the inserting key evicts the key with the shorter probe distance ('stealing from the rich'). Result: variance in probe lengths is minimized — lookup is faster in practice.
characteristics: Insert: steal slots from entries with shorter probe distances
Result: max probe distance is bounded — very uniform distribution
Deletion: backward-shift deletion avoids tombstones
Used in Rust HashMap (hashbrown) and modern high-performance tables
useCases: High-performance systems requiring predictable lookup time

files.code:
# ─── Hash Table in Python ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#
# Python's dict is a highly optimised hash table using open addressing
# with pseudo-random probing (not linear). It resizes at load factor 2/3.
# Keys must be hashable (immutable: str, int, tuple — not list or dict).

# ── Built-in dict ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
d = {}
d['name'] = 'Alice'      # O(1) insert
d['age']  = 30           # O(1) insert
d.get('name')            # O(1) → 'Alice'
'age' in d               # O(1) → True
del d['age']             # O(1)
len(d)                   # O(1) → 1

# Dict comprehension
squares = {x: x**2 for x in range(5)}  # {0:0, 1:1, 2:4, 3:9, 4:16}

# setdefault — insert only if key absent
d.setdefault('city', 'NYC')   # inserts; returns 'NYC'
d.setdefault('name', 'Bob')   # key exists; returns 'Alice'

# Counter — frequency map (hash table with default 0)
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
counts  = Counter('aabbbc')         # {'b':3, 'a':2, 'c':1}
defmap  = defaultdict(list)          # missing keys default to []

# ── Manual chaining hash table ────────────────────────────────────────────
class HashMap:
    def __init__(self, capacity=16):
        self.capacity  = capacity
        self.buckets   = [[] for _ in range(capacity)]
        self.size      = 0
        self.THRESHOLD = 0.75

    def _hash(self, key):
        h = 0
        for ch in str(key):
            h = (h * 31 + ord(ch)) % self.capacity
        return h

    def set(self, key, value):                      # O(1) avg
        idx   = self._hash(key)
        chain = self.buckets[idx]
        for entry in chain:
            if entry[0] == key:
                entry[1] = value; return
        chain.append([key, value])
        self.size += 1
        if self.size / self.capacity > self.THRESHOLD:
            self._resize()

    def get(self, key):                             # O(1) avg
        idx = self._hash(key)
        for k, v in self.buckets[idx]:
            if k == key: return v
        return None

    def contains(self, key):                        # O(1) avg
        return self.get(key) is not None

    def delete(self, key):                          # O(1) avg
        idx = self._hash(key)
        chain = self.buckets[idx]
        for i, (k, _) in enumerate(chain):
            if k == key:
                chain.pop(i); self.size -= 1; return True
        return False

    def _resize(self):                              # O(n)
        old = self.buckets
        self.capacity *= 2
        self.buckets   = [[] for _ in range(self.capacity)]
        self.size      = 0
        for chain in old:
            for k, v in chain:
                self.set(k, v)

    @property
    def load_factor(self): return self.size / self.capacity

footerAlerts.title: Chaining vs open addressing — when to use each
items: Separate chaining — prefer when load factor may exceed 0.7, deletion is frequent, or key size is large. Memory overhead: one pointer per bucket plus linked list nodes.

Open addressing (linear probing) — prefer when cache performance matters, keys are small integers or fixed-size, and load factor is kept below 0.7. All data in one contiguous array = fewer cache misses.
Robin Hood hashing (open addressing variant) — best practical performance. Used in Rust's HashMap. Equalises probe distances, eliminating worst-case hot spots.
For concurrent access: use ConcurrentHashMap (Java) or lock striping — never a global lock on the whole table.

footerAlerts.title: When hash tables shine
items: Frequency counting — count occurrences in O(n): map[key]++ for each element.
Two sum / complement lookup — store seen values, check complement in O(1) per element.
Deduplication — insert into a hash set and check .has() before processing.
Caching / memoization — O(1) lookup of previously computed results.
Graph adjacency representation — vertex → neighbor list mapping.

footerAlerts.title: Common interview pitfalls
items: Mutable keys — using a list or array as a hash map key causes errors. Keys must be immutable and hashable.
Assuming O(1) worst case — hash tables are O(n) worst case. For guaranteed O(log n), use a balanced BST (TreeMap).
Tombstone accumulation in open addressing — too many deleted slots degrade performance. Periodic rehash clears them.
Java: HashMap vs HashSet — HashSet<T> is just a HashMap<T, Object> wrapper. Same complexity, different API.
Forgetting to handle collisions in custom implementations — always test with keys that hash to the same bucket.

hints: The load factor threshold matters: chaining can handle load > 1 (chains grow), but open addressing requires load < 1 (table would be full). Java defaults to 0.75 for chaining.
For the 'contains duplicate' pattern: insert into a set and check size before and after — or check .has() before .add().
When you need O(1) insert and O(log n) kth-largest: combine a hash map with a heap — this is the design of LRU Cache.
